justification: This large-scale usability study of AI programming assistants identifies concrete functional and non‑functional needs — e.g., adherence to functional/non‑functional constraints (security, performance, style), controllability, provenance/explanations, context‑awareness, personalization/feedback, and interaction modes — that directly inform requirements for agentic AI assistants. However, the study targets software developers and code generation use cases rather than data or natural scientists specifically, so applicability requires domain adaptation.

Highly relevant — the paper explicitly defines functional requirements (agent group roles for planning/execution, tool invocation, knowledge-base retrieval, code generation, human–agent interaction and workflow decomposition) and non-functional requirements (stability/reliability, hallucination mitigation, scalability, resource efficiency, maintainability and continuous learning). It also provides evaluations and ablation studies in large-scale omics workflows, giving concrete design and assessment guidance transferable to agentic AI for data and natural scientists.

The paper enumerates concrete functional modules (RL core, human-feedback interface, bias detector, XAI, fuzzy reasoning) and non-functional concerns (interpretability, real-time adaptability, fairness, low latency, trust) that map to requirements for agentic AI, but it is grounded in intelligent retail rather than tools targeted specifically at supporting data scientists or natural scientists, limiting direct domain transferability.
